perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall pay to Fox, or such other party as Fox may designate in writing, a royalty in the following amount: (a) From the first unit sold, Fox shall earn a royalty at the rate of ***** percent (*****%) of Licensee's Gross Receipts (as defined in the Agreement) from Licensee's sale and distribution of the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall endeavor to pay Fox, or such other party as Fox may designate in writing, a royalty in the following amount: (a) From the first unit sold, Fox may earn a royalty at approximately ***** percent (*****%) of Licensee's Gross Receipts (as defined in the Agreement) from Licensee's sale and distribution of the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'shall pay' to 'shall endeavor to pay' and 'shall earn' to 'may earn', replacing a mandatory obligation with a discretionary one and creating ambiguity around the royalty rate with 'approximately'.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1636 states that a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. The original text indicated a clear intention to pay royalties. The modified text undermines this clear intention by introducing ambiguity regarding both the obligation to pay and the specific amount due, making the obligation unenforceable under normal contract law principles.
perturbation.location: B. IA 2 AUDIO AND VIDEO WIRELESS PRODUCTS ROYALTY: 1. Royalty:
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1636. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the obligation to pay royalties and the specific amount due. The original contract language 'shall pay' and 'shall earn' indicates a clear, mandatory obligation. In contrast, the changed text, 'shall endeavor to pay' and 'may earn approximately,' creates uncertainty about whether the licensee is actually obligated to pay royalties and the exact amount. This undermines the clear intention to pay royalties. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing California Civil Code § 1636, states that contracts should be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties at the time of contracting, and the modified language obscures the original intention, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: All VGSL and other CSP payments remitted directly to Fox pursuant to the VGSL Agreement and other CSP agreements with respect to the IA Audio and Video Wireless Products shall be treated in the same manner as set forth in Paragraphs 7(b)(ii) and 7(b)(iii) of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: VGSL and other CSP payments remitted directly to Fox pursuant to the VGSL Agreement and other CSP agreements with respect to the IA Audio and Video Wireless Products might be treated in a similar manner to that set forth in Paragraphs 7(b)(ii) and 7(b)(iii) of the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be treated' to 'might be treated' and 'same manner' to 'similar manner' introduces significant ambiguity and makes the handling of VGSL payments discretionary. This creates a potentially unenforceable obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2204 states a contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract. By making the payment processing discretionary, the modification obscures a previously clear term, and can create a situation where the contract lacks a clear and enforceable standard for dealing with those payments.
perturbation.location: B. IA 2 AUDIO AND VIDEO WIRELESS PRODUCTS ROYALTY: 2. VGSL and other CSP Accounting:
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2204 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract [2201 - 2210] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2204. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract. (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall be treated' to 'might be treated' introduces significant ambiguity regarding payment processing, directly contradicting the original text's intent. The Law Explanation indicates that by making payment processing discretionary, the modification creates a situation where the contract lacks a clear and enforceable standard. While the scraped law snippet discusses contract formation generally, the Perturbation Explanation states the modified text creates an unenforceable obligation, fulfilling the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition, where the obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall not be entitled to receive a share of any revenues and shall have no claim to any revenues earned and collected by Fox, or a third-party Fox designates, for the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products which Fox distributes through its and its assigns' direct-to-consumer distribution channels.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee should not generally be entitled to receive a share of revenues, and will usually have no claim to revenues earned and collected by Fox, or a third-party Fox designates, for the IA2 Audio and Video Wireless Products which Fox distributes through its and its assigns' direct-to-consumer distribution channels.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not' with 'should not generally' and 'shall have no claim' with 'will usually have no claim' weakens the prohibition and introduces ambiguity, making the licensee's entitlement to revenue a matter of interpretation rather than a definitive exclusion.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1644 states that the words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. The original text clearly excludes the licensee from revenue sharing. The modified text opens the possibility of claims in unspecified situations, contradicting the original intention of a clear exclusion.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1644 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1644. The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text employs definitive language ('shall not,' 'shall have no claim') to prohibit the licensee from receiving revenue shares. The modified text weakens this prohibition by using terms like 'should not generally' and 'will usually have no claim,' which introduces ambiguity and opens the door for potential claims in unspecified situations. This directly contradicts the original intention of a clear exclusion, as indicated by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically California Civil Code § 1644, states that the words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense. The change from 'shall not' to 'should not generally' alters the ordinary and popular sense of the clause, moving it from a definitive prohibition to a conditional one, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined in the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition. The ambiguous language makes the obligation difficult to enforce.
